text: # The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: [Interview](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/interviews/index.html)

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: [Leisure](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/leisure/index.html)

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: [Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Rising costs, higher tourist taxes and an impending VAT increase from 9 to 21% on overnight stays: the leisure sector is facing some major challenges. More than ever, survival and success depend on creativity and “price-through power,” says sector economist Gerarda Westerhuis of ABN AMRO. 'Customers are looking for experience and above all want value for their money'.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: 'Purchasing power has increased, but we are not spending it in droves. We remain cautious', signals Gerarda Westerhuis, Leisure sector economist at ABN AMRO, based on the most recent economic figures. 'We still save a lot, even though we have more to spend. You see this double in the catering industry: people are not gloomy, but they are more selective. They want value for their money.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: According to her, this nuance characterizes the leisure sector in 2025: cautious optimism, but no exuberance. The pandemic years are behind us, tourists are returning, and purchasing power is increasing thanks to higher wages and government measures. Yet trust is fragile. 'Building memories has become more important than buying things. People continue to travel, eat out and spend the day away. Only: more conscious. The choice for quality and experience wins over quantity and that requires a different sharpness from entrepreneurs than in the years of abundance.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: > “The demand for long tours through the US has decreased. At the same time, you see that European destinations are benefiting.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: After the erratic corona years, the growth of the sector is normalizing. For 2025, ABN AMRO expects volume growth of 1.5%. Household consumption is increasing by about 1% and the number of foreign tourists is growing to 22 million, more than 4% more than last year. The domestic market also remains stable with more than 30 million Dutch guests (source: NBTC). For example, campsites saw the number of overnight stays grow by 1% in the first half of 2025, while holiday parks remained stuck at 0%. Hotels actually grew 2%. It is striking that the number of bookings from the Netherlands decreased by 2%, but from abroad increased by 4%, with the majority of foreign guests coming from Germany (20%), followed by Belgium (4%). 'And Southern Europeans are also increasingly finding their way to our country.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: World politics subtly affects travel behavior. Westerhuis sees that people prefer to wait and see. 'They book shorter in advance and stay a little closer to home. This makes it more difficult to plan for entrepreneurs, but also offers room for those who can respond flexibly to last-minute needs.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: The risks are greater for travel organizations with a large offering in America. 'The demand for long tours through the US has decreased. At the same time, you see that European destinations are benefiting: Lisbon, Prague, Amsterdam, Scandinavia. The wanderlust remains, but the direction changes.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: ## Keep running healthily by offering something special

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Although the leisure sector certainly faces headwinds, from staff shortages and rising costs to VAT increases, entrepreneur Dirk Lips from Libéma (including Beekse Bergen) sees plenty of opportunities. 'The demand for short holidays close to home remains high. People want to get out and are willing to pay for something special. Then you have to make sure that you have something special to offer.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: According to Lips, that specialness lies in distinctive entrepreneurship. 'You should not point fingers at the government or the economy, but remain creative and sharp. Make sure your product is unique, that you deliver quality, and that your hospitality is top notch. And that you know your company's figures down to line level. That is entrepreneurship.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: The growth in turnover masks the fact that profit margins are thin. Personnel, rent, purchasing and energy became significantly more expensive. Contract wages have increased by 25.5% since 2020; coffee was more than 22% more expensive in September 2025 than in September last year, beef and veal 33.5% and chocolate 19% (source: CBS, ABN AMRO). On top of that, there will be higher taxes: an increase in the tourist tax in many municipalities and the planned VAT increase from 9 to 21% on overnight stays as of 2026.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: “This VAT increase will really be a test for the sector,” says Westerhuis. 'Hotels and holiday parks in the middle segment have little room to pass on the higher rates. This works better in the luxury segment, because the guest there is less price sensitive. Pricing power is the new distinction: those who have it can invest and grow; those who don't have it must look for efficiency and new value.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Compared to neighboring countries, the competitive position is deteriorating. Belgium and Germany apply VAT rates of 6 and 7% respectively on overnight stays. “That makes a big difference,” Westerhuis warns. 'This can be decisive, especially with international guests. Entrepreneurs will have to look more closely at costs, energy consumption and staff deployment to maintain margins.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: > “Smart systems can make tasks easier, but hospitality remains a human job.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: A striking shift is taking place in food service. Fast service companies – the fast food and delivery chains – grew rapidly during and after corona, but that growth is stalling. Fast food prices increased by almost 40% between January 2020 and June 2025, compared to 28% in restaurants and 34% in supermarkets. “Even fast food is no longer necessarily cheap,” says Westerhuis. 'The price advantage has disappeared. As a result, people nowadays think more quickly: for that money I could also cook for myself or eat better food. You see that quick service companies are conducting more promotions and expanding their menus with healthier or more luxurious options. They are looking for their place again.' The shift also affects restaurants in the middle segment. Consumers come slightly less often, but spend more on average per visit. 'Here too, the focus is more on experience and quality. Those who can offer that will survive – those who cannot will disappear into the crowd.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: The shortage on the labor market remains severe, despite a slight decrease in the number of vacancies. In the second quarter of 2025, there were still 28,400 vacancies open in the catering industry; More than 40% of entrepreneurs mention staff shortages as an obstacle (source: CBS). “Many entrepreneurs in this sector rely on students and part-time workers,” says Westerhuis. 'That provides flexibility, but also makes it vulnerable; the outflow is high and the flow to permanent jobs is limited. If you want to retain staff, you must invest in career paths, training and flexibility. Young people want clarity and appreciation, not just hours behind the bar.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Although automation can alleviate some of that pressure, she says technology remains a means, not a solution. 'Hospitality is and remains human work. Smart systems can make tasks easier, but the difference is made by good staff who feel involved.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: A silent trend in the sector is increasing scale. According to Westerhuis, entrepreneurs look for security by working together or joining larger chains. 'Scale is a buffer against volatility. You can purchase better, deploy staff more flexibly and invest in technology. We will see more clustering, both in hotels and in the catering industry. Small self-employed people remain important, but the margins force cooperation. This does not always have to be an acquisition – joint purchasing or marketing can also yield a lot.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: > “Sustainability is not only a moral choice, it is also a cost saving.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Despite the pressure, Westerhuis sees plenty of opportunities. 'There will be air again. The demand is there, purchasing power is increasing and the sector is learning to plan better.' According to her, the key to further growth lies in the ability of entrepreneurs to find new markets, use resources more intelligently and retain customers for the long term.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: A first growth path lies in the shifting target groups. The expensive dollar means that fewer Americans are coming to the Netherlands, while southern Europeans are finding their way north now that heat waves are increasingly making their summers too difficult. The aging population also offers opportunities. According to her, the over-55s are one of the most stable pillars of the sector: 'They travel more quietly and throughout the year. This helps entrepreneurs to spread the seasons better.' The domestic meeting market complements this development. 'Conferences and events are back to normal. This creates year-round employment and makes personnel planning easier.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: A second source of growth is sustainability, which, according to Westerhuis, increasingly goes hand in hand with efficiency. 'Sustainability is not only a moral choice, it is also a cost saving. A good monitoring system for energy consumption or inventory management quickly pays for itself. Entrepreneurs who limit their waste flows, combat food waste or invest in energy-efficient installations see this immediately reflected in their margins.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: In addition, the role of technology is increasing rapidly. Digitization and data analysis make it possible to better predict when guests will book, how many staff are needed and what prices are feasible.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Finally, she sees a growing value in loyalty and commitment. Consumers want to recognize themselves in the place they visit and are more likely to choose brands that appeal to them personally. 'Loyalty programs, annual cards or local communities work well. It's no longer just about discounts, but about connection. People want to feel part of something that suits them.' Together, she believes, these developments form the new foundation for growth in the leisure sector: dealing smarter with people and resources, responding better to changing behavior and at the same time strengthening the bond with the guest. “Anyone who combines this,” Westerhuis concludes, “builds resilience – and that is becoming increasingly important in this sector.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: > “Growth does not happen by itself; it is the result of smart choices.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: According to Westerhuis, the leisure sector is in the next phase. 'The enormous peaks and valleys of the corona period are over. We are moving to a more stable, but more demanding market. Growth does not come naturally; it is the result of smart choices.' These choices differ per segment. Large chains benefit from economies of scale in purchasing, personnel and technology. Small entrepreneurs win with authenticity, local ties and experience. “Anyone who really knows the guest always has an advantage,” Westerhuis notes.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: And then there is the VAT increase in 2026. “That will be a reality check,” she predicts. 'Companies that invest now in efficiency, personnel and digitalization will be able to overcome this hurdle better. Anyone who waits runs the risk of being overtaken. The entrepreneurs who have survived the corona crisis are financially more careful, but often strategically stronger. That resilience is their greatest asset.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: ## Providing our guests with a good time is what it's all about

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Lips finds the announced VAT increase from 9 to 21% and the rising tourist tax “worrisome”. 'If you cannot pass this on, it quickly costs half or more of your net margin. While you need it for maintenance, personnel and innovation. Moreover, it makes the Netherlands more expensive than our neighboring countries. If you want to increase taxes, do it gradually, so that entrepreneurs can move along.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: Lips himself is investing heavily, especially now. 'In Beekse Bergen, guests can literally sleep among the giraffes and zebras - and soon also among the elephants. There is no second place in Europe where that is possible. With such distinctive concepts you can stay healthy - even if costs rise.' He is optimistic: by 2030 he wants to grow from the current € 208 million turnover - with 1,500 employees, 860,000 overnight stays and 4.7 million day visitors - to € 300 million. 'If you are on your feet and continue to innovate, there is every reason to be optimistic. Providing our guests with a good time is what it's all about.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: > “You should not point fingers at the government or the economy, but remain creative and sharp yourself. That is entrepreneurship.”

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: There is still discussion in Parliament whether the planned VAT increase on overnight stays will actually lead to a tax benefit and therefore whether the increase should be continued. If the increase does not go ahead, ABN AMRO expects volume growth of 1% for the sector by 2026. If the planned increase does go ahead, a decrease is expected. The foundations are solid, but the margin remains thin. “Growing in leisure is not a sprint but a marathon,” Westerhuis concludes. 'Those who price through where possible, reduce costs where necessary and spread demand across target groups and seasons, maintain momentum. The sector has proven to be resilient. Now it is important to maintain that resilience.'

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: From cost savings and digitalization with AI: the leisure sector has a positive outlook and is undergoing important developments. In this video, business banker Aljan van Ruiten explains where your growth opportunities lie in 2026.

metadata.source_title: The leisure sector: cautious optimism and major challenges

text: The Leisure sector has largely gone through a difficult period. The recovery has started with the phasing out of the measures regarding corona. The terraces are starting to fill up, the number of tourists is increasing and events can be organized again. Staff shortages are now the biggest challenge. Digitizing staff work and spreading guests can limit the impact.
